title: GEDCOM to Pedigree Analysis
text: core research question one paternity. William Pitt Waugh (b. 28 April 1775, Adams County, Pennsylvania; d. 14 August 1852, Wilkes County, North Carolina) bachelor slaveholder. Historical records show placed “Runaway/Fugitive Enslaved Person” notice Alexandria Gazette 21 March 1820. last testament, filed Wilkes County, names lawful wife. Evidence tree points two sons born two different women color — outside marriage: W. Henderson Waugh (b. approximately 1835, Wilkes County NC), son Matilda Grinton. 1860 census lists Matilda’s occupation “House Woman.” W. Henderson married Laura Watkins 24 June 1877 direct paternal ancestor focal individual. William Pitt Waugh Jr. (b. 1844, Wilkes County NC), born William Segraves, son Martha Law Segraves. birth note reads: “Born wedlock Martha Law Segraves. Uses surname 1865. Purported Natural son William P Waugh (1775–1852).” served Civil War “William Segraves, Company E, First Regiment Middle Tennessee Infantry,” took Waugh name war. explicitly excluded William Pitt Waugh’s : “make bequest purported natural son Martha Laws, William Segraves.” W. Henderson Waugh William Pitt Waugh Jr. , documentary evidence correct, paternal half-brothers — sons William Pitt Waugh Sr. different mothers. question whether shared Y chromosome can confirmed genetic genealogy. paternal chain focal individual back history : Generation | Individual | Birth | |||| | Self | Christian Emil WAUGH | 29 Apr 1978, Monterey County CA | | Father | Gregory Emil Waugh | Jun 1958 | | Grandfather | Warner F Waugh | abt 1933, NC | | Great-grandfather | John William “Bud” Waugh | abt Jun 1880, NC | | 2nd great-grandfather | W. Henderson Waugh | abt 1835, NC | | 3rd great-grandfather | William Pitt Waugh Sr. | 28 Apr 1775, Adams County PA | W. Henderson Waugh’s paternity rests historical record rather DNA, Y-chromosome match living male descendant William Pitt Waugh Jr. (Segraves line) strongly corroborate William Pitt Waugh Sr. fathered half-brothers. Furthermore, presence Y-chromosome match living male line descent brother William Pitt Waugh Sr. also support paternal link W. Henderson Waugh William Pitt Waugh Sr., well broader family tree.

what: Working with a real GEDCOM file
title: GEDCOM to Pedigree Analysis
text: apply workflow real Ancestry.com export: Note Ancestry.com regenerates person IDs time export, ID values differ exports. Always locate individuals name birth date rather hardcoded ID.
code: # Unzip the Ancestry export unzip("W. Henderson Waugh Family Tree.zip", overwrite = TRUE)  # Peek at the raw file to orient yourself before parsing raw_ged <- readLines("W. Henderson Waugh Family Tree.ged") head(raw_ged, 15)  # Locate the anchor individual by name line_num <- which(grepl("1 NAME W. Henderson /Waugh/", raw_ged, fixed = TRUE)) raw_ged[line_num:(line_num + 20)]  # Parse all INDI blocks ped <- readGedcom("W. Henderson Waugh Family Tree.ged",   remove_empty_cols = TRUE, verbose = FALSE )  # Add year columns robust to approximate dates ped$birth_year <- extractGedcomYear(ped$birth_date) ped$death_year <- extractGedcomYear(ped$death_date)  # Quick overview summarizeGedcom(ped)  # Parse family/marriage records fam <- readGedcomFamilies("W. Henderson Waugh Family Tree.ged", verbose = FALSE)

title: GEDCOM to Pedigree Analysis
text: data tidy data frame, BGmisc package provides next layer analysis: computing pairwise relatedness, tracing paternal lineages, identifying Y-chromosome carriers. strategy Waugh paternity question : Identify focal individual trace Y-chromosome line back William Pitt Waugh Sr. Flag individuals descended William Pitt Waugh Sr. W. Henderson Waugh’s branch — descendants William Pitt Waugh Jr. (Segraves line). Among , find living males: carry Y chromosome William Pitt Waugh Sr. potential DNA match candidates. Y-chromosome match living Segraves-line male focal individual (close Y-line relatives) strongly confirm W. Henderson Waugh William Pitt Waugh Jr. share father.  plot shows branches descending William Pitt Waugh Sr.: green (focal) line W. Henderson Waugh left, Segraves branch William Pitt Waugh Jr. right. distinct people distinct IDs — shared surname makes tree appear circular first glance, loop. full Waugh analysis, addPaternalChain() addPaternalLineFlag() BGmisc extend flagging descendants specific ancestors. key variable : isolates individuals descended William Pitt Waugh Sr. W. Henderson Waugh — .e., Segraves (William Pitt Jr.) branch — primary Y-DNA match candidates. ggPedigreeInteractive() renders full tree nodes colored additive genetic relatedness focal individual edges highlighted patriline membership.

Henderson; great-grandfather of focal person   "0 @I7@ INDI",   "1 NAME John William /Waugh/",   "1 SEX M",   "1 BIRT",   "2 DATE ABT JUN 1880",   "2 PLAC North Carolina, USA",   "1 FAMC @F3@",    # James Monroe Waugh — son of William Pitt Jr.; Y-DNA candidate branch   "0 @I8@ INDI",   "1 NAME James Monroe /Waugh/",   "1 SEX M",   "1 BIRT",   "2 DATE 10 NOV 1867",   "1 DEAT",   "2 DATE 23 JUL 1937",   "1 FAMC @F4@",    # Family 1: William Pitt Sr. + Matilda Grinton -> W. Henderson Waugh   "0 @F1@ FAM",   "1 HUSB @I1@",   "1 WIFE @I2@",   "1 CHIL @I3@",    # Family 2: William Pitt Sr. + Martha Segraves -> William Pitt Jr.   # _SREL friend marks this as a non-marital relationship in Ancestry exports   "0 @F2@ FAM",   "1 HUSB @I1@",   "1 WIFE @I4@",   "1 CHIL @I5@",   "1 _SREL friend",    # Family 3: W. Henderson Waugh + Laura Watkins   "0 @F3@ FAM",   "1 HUSB @I3@",   "1 WIFE @I6@",   "1 CHIL @I7@",   "1 MARR",   "2 DATE 24 JUN 1877",   "2 PLAC Wilkes County, North Carolina, USA",    # Family 4: William Pitt Jr. + wife   "0 @F4@ FAM",   "1 HUSB @I5@",   "1 CHIL @I8@",   "0 TRLR" )  tmp_ged <- tempfile(fileext = ".ged") writeLines(sample_ged, tmp_ged) ped <- readGedcom(tmp_ged, verbose = FALSE)  # Convert IDs to numeric (required by BGmisc) ped_num <- ped ped_num$personID <- as.numeric(ped_num$personID) ped_num$momID <- as.numeric(ped_num$momID) ped_num$dadID <- as.numeric(ped_num$dadID)  # Structure pedigree into family units and compute paternal line IDs ged_ped <- ped2fam(ped_num, personID = "personID") |>   ped2paternal(personID = "personID", momID = "momID", dadID = "dadID")  # Identify John William "Bud" Waugh as the focal person's great-grandfather # (the deepest ancestor in our sample with a known paternal chain) focal_ID <- ged_ped$personID[grepl("John William", ged_ped$name)] y_line_ID <- ged_ped$patID[ged_ped$personID == focal_ID]  # Flag Y-line membership — green branch = focal person's line ged_ped$y_line <- ifelse(ged_ped$patID == y_line_ID, "Y Line", "Other Lines") ggpedigree(   ged_ped,   personID = "personID",   momID = "momID",   dadID = "dadID",   sexVar = "sex",   config = list(     label_include = TRUE,     label_column = "name",     label_text_size = 2,     code_male = "M",     code_female = "F",     segment_lineage_include = TRUE,     segment_lineage_focal_personID = focal_ID,     segment_lineage_component = "paternal",     segment_lineage_legend_title = "Patriline",     add_phantoms = TRUE,     founder_order_seed = 1L   ) ) #> Warning in buildPlotConfig(default_config = default_config, config = config, : #> The following config values are not recognized by getDefaultPlotConfig(): #> segment_lineage_include, segment_lineage_focal_personID, #> segment_lineage_component, segment_lineage_legend_title, founder_order_seed #> REPAIR IN EARLY ALPHA in_granddad_henderson_line_not_dad_henderson_line =   in_granddad_henderson_line & !in_dad_henderson_line

title: Getting Started with tidygedcom
text: GEDCOM (Genealogical Data Communication) plain-text file format used virtually every genealogy platform — Ancestry, FamilySearch, MyHeritage, Gramps, others. typical workflow looks like : Build find family tree Ancestry.com (similar). Export .ged file tree settings page. Load R readGedcom(). file structured text file. small slice: Individual records (INDI) hold person-level facts; family records (FAM) link spouses children carry marriage divorce events.
code: 0 @I1@ INDI 1 NAME William Pitt /Waugh/ 1 SEX M 1 BIRT 2 DATE 28 APR 1775 2 PLAC Adams County, Pennsylvania, USA 1 DEAT 2 DATE 14 AUG 1852 2 PLAC Wilkes County, North Carolina, USA 1 FAMS @F28@ 1 FAMS @F29@  0 @F28@ FAM 1 HUSB @I1@ 1 WIFE @I2@ 1 CHIL @I3@  0 @F29@ FAM 1 HUSB @I1@ 1 WIFE @I4@ 1 CHIL @I5@

title: Getting Started with tidygedcom
text: tidygedcom package provides tools parsing tidying GEDCOM files, making easier work genealogical data R. R’s powerful data manipulation capabilities allow researchers clean, analyze, visualize genealogical data ways may possible traditional genealogy software. Additionally, R’s extensive ecosystem packages statistical analysis machine learning can applied genealogical data uncover patterns insights may immediately apparent. vignette uses real family tree running example: W. Henderson Waugh Family Tree. ’ll discuss running example another vignette. (briefly, Waugh family tree genetic genealogy collaboration two Wake Forest psychology faculty – SMG CEW. See https://www.arcadiapublishing.com/products/slavery--wilkes-county-north-carolina-9781467135832 historical context Waugh family tree.) tidygedcom package provides tools parsing tidying GEDCOM files, Waugh family tree provides real-world test case tools.

code: sample_ged <- c(   "0 HEAD",   "1 GEDC",   "2 VERS 5.5.1",   "1 CHAR UTF-8",    # William Pitt Waugh Sr. — the common paternal ancestor   "0 @I1@ INDI",   "1 NAME William Pitt /Waugh/",   "1 SEX M",   "1 BIRT",   "2 DATE 28 APR 1775",   "2 PLAC Adams County, Pennsylvania, USA",   "1 DEAT",   "2 DATE 14 AUG 1852",   "2 PLAC Wilkes County, North Carolina, USA",   "1 FAMS @F1@",   "1 FAMS @F2@",    # Matilda Grinton — mother of W. Henderson Waugh   "0 @I2@ INDI",   "1 NAME Matilda /Grinton/",   "1 SEX F",   "1 BIRT",   "2 DATE ABT 1797",   "2 PLAC North Carolina, USA",   "1 FAMS @F1@",    # W. Henderson Waugh — 2nd great-grandfather of focal person   "0 @I3@ INDI",   "1 NAME W. Henderson /Waugh/",   "1 SEX M",   "1 BIRT",   "2 DATE ABT 1835",   "2 PLAC Wilkes County, North Carolina, USA",   "1 FAMC @F1@",   "1 FAMS @F3@",    # Martha Law Segraves — mother of William Pitt Waugh Jr.   "0 @I4@ INDI",   "1 NAME Martha Law /Segraves/",   "1 SEX F",   "1 BIRT",   "2 DATE OCT 1814",   "1 FAMS @F2@",    # William Pitt Waugh Jr. (born William Segraves) — paternal half-brother of W. Henderson   "0 @I5@ INDI",   "1 NAME William Pitt /Waugh/ Jr.",   "1 SEX M",   "1 BIRT",   "2 DATE 1844",   "2 PLAC Wilkes County, North Carolina, USA",   "1 DEAT",   "2 DATE FEB 1880",   "1 FAMC @F2@",   "1 FAMS @F4@",    # Laura Watkins — wife of W. Henderson Waugh   "0 @I6@ INDI",   "1 NAME Laura /Watkins/",   "1 SEX F",   "1 BIRT",   "2 DATE ABT 1846",   "2 PLAC North Carolina, USA",   "1 FAMS @F3@",    # John William (Bud) Waugh — son of W. Henderson Waugh   "0 @F1@ FAM",   "1 HUSB @I1@",   "1 WIFE @I2@",   "1 CHIL @I3@",    # Family 2: William Pitt Sr. + Martha Segraves -> William Pitt Jr.   # _SREL friend marks this as a non-marital relationship in Ancestry exports   "0 @F2@ FAM",   "1 HUSB @I1@",   "1 WIFE @I4@",   "1 CHIL @I5@",   "1 _SREL friend",    # Family 3: W. Henderson Waugh + Laura Watkins   "0 @F3@ FAM",   "1 HUSB @I3@",   "1 WIFE @I6@",   "1 CHIL @I7@",   "1 MARR",   "2 DATE 24 JUN 1877",   "2 PLAC Wilkes County, North Carolina, USA",    # Family 4: William Pitt Jr. + wife   "0 @F4@ FAM",   "1 HUSB @I5@",   "1 CHIL @I8@",   "0 TRLR" )  tmp_ged <- tempfile(fileext = ".ged") writeLines(sample_ged, tmp_ged)

text: readGedcom() parses INDI blocks returns one row per person. infers momID dadID automatically tracing FAMC → FAMS links — manual ID mapping required. Notice W. Henderson Waugh William Pitt Waugh Jr. share dadID — William Pitt Waugh Sr. — reflecting half-sibling relationship parsed data.
code: ped <- readGedcom(tmp_ged, verbose = FALSE) ped[, c("personID", "name", "sex", "birth_date", "death_date", "momID", "dadID")] #>   personID                    name sex   birth_date  death_date momID dadID #> 1        1      William Pitt Waugh   M  28 APR 1775 14 AUG 1852  <NA>  <NA> #> 2        2         Matilda Grinton   F     ABT 1797        <NA>  <NA>  <NA> #> 3        3      W. Henderson Waugh   M     ABT 1835        <NA>     2     1 #> 4        4     Martha Law Segraves   F     OCT 1814        <NA>  <NA>  <NA> #> 5        5 William Pitt Waugh/ Jr.   M         1844    FEB 1880     4     1 #> 6        6           Laura Watkins   F     ABT 1846        <NA>  <NA>  <NA> #> 7        7      John William Waugh   M ABT JUN 1880        <NA>     6     3 #> 8        8      James Monroe Waugh   M  10 NOV 1867 23 JUL 1937  <NA>     5

title: Getting Started with tidygedcom
text: GEDCOM dates raw strings often carry qualifiers (ABT, BEF, AFT) calendar escape codes (@#DGREGORIAN@). Historical genealogy — especially antebellum American records — full approximate dates: birth years reconstructed census age questions, death years inferred probate filings, marriage dates county clerk records never systematically kept.

code: ped_dates <- readGedcom(tmp_ged, parse_dates = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) ped_dates[, c("name", "birth_date", "death_date")] #>                      name birth_date death_date #> 1      William Pitt Waugh 1775-04-28 1852-08-14 #> 2         Matilda Grinton       <NA>       <NA> #> 3      W. Henderson Waugh       <NA>       <NA> #> 4     Martha Law Segraves       <NA>       <NA> #> 5 William Pitt Waugh/ Jr.       <NA>       <NA> #> 6           Laura Watkins       <NA>       <NA> #> 7      John William Waugh       <NA>       <NA> #> 8      James Monroe Waugh 1867-11-10 1937-07-23

what: Extract a year from any GEDCOM date string
title: Getting Started with tidygedcom
text: dates parse cleanly Date objects — "ABT 1835" "OCT 1814" day component. extractGedcomYear() handles forms returns integer year: typical use: add birth_year column robust approximate dates, flag individuals may still living:
code: dates_raw <- c(   "28 APR 1775", "14 AUG 1852", "ABT 1835", "OCT 1814",   "1844", "ABT JUN 1880", NA ) extractGedcomYear(dates_raw) #> [1] 1775 1852 1835 1814 1844 1880   NA ped$birth_year <- extractGedcomYear(ped$birth_date) ped$death_year <- extractGedcomYear(ped$death_date) ped[, c("name", "birth_year", "death_year")] #>                      name birth_year death_year #> 1      William Pitt Waugh       1775       1852 #> 2         Matilda Grinton       1797         NA #> 3      W. Henderson Waugh       1835         NA #> 4     Martha Law Segraves       1814         NA #> 5 William Pitt Waugh/ Jr.       1844       1880 #> 6           Laura Watkins       1846         NA #> 7      John William Waugh       1880         NA #> 8      James Monroe Waugh       1867       1937

title: Getting Started with tidygedcom
text: Marriage relationship data live FAM blocks. readGedcomFamilies() parses separate data frame: _SREL friend tag Family 2 — non-marital relationship William Pitt Waugh Sr. Martha Law Segraves — preserved raw GEDCOM yet parsed structured column. known limitation working Ancestry.com exports use non-standard tags. Join back individuals table annotate families spouse names:
code: fam <- readGedcomFamilies(tmp_ged, verbose = FALSE) fam[, c("famID", "husbID", "wifeID", "marr_date", "marr_place")] #>   famID husbID wifeID   marr_date                         marr_place #> 1     1      1      2        <NA>                               <NA> #> 2     2      1      4        <NA>                               <NA> #> 3     3      3      6 24 JUN 1877 Wilkes County, North Carolina, USA #> 4     4      5   <NA>        <NA>                               <NA> merge(   fam[, c("famID", "husbID", "wifeID", "marr_date", "marr_place")],   ped[, c("personID", "name")],   by.x = "husbID", by.y = "personID", all.x = TRUE ) |>   merge(     ped[, c("personID", "name")],     by.x = "wifeID", by.y = "personID", all.x = TRUE,     suffixes = c("_husb", "_wife")   ) #>   wifeID husbID famID   marr_date                         marr_place #> 1      2      1     1        <NA>                               <NA> #> 2      4      1     2        <NA>                               <NA> #> 3      6      3     3 24 JUN 1877 Wilkes County, North Carolina, USA #> 4   <NA>      5     4        <NA>                               <NA> #>                 name_husb           name_wife #> 1      William Pitt Waugh     Matilda Grinton #> 2      William Pitt Waugh Martha Law Segraves #> 3      W. Henderson Waugh       Laura Watkins #> 4 William Pitt Waugh/ Jr.                <NA>

title: Getting Started with tidygedcom
text: GEDCOM stores coordinates compass-prefix notation (N36.1234, W81.5678). convertGedcomCoords() converts _lat/_long columns signed decimal degrees one call: can also call underlying converters directly:
code: coord_ged <- c(   "0 HEAD", "1 GEDC", "2 VERS 5.5.1", "1 CHAR UTF-8",   "0 @I1@ INDI",   "1 NAME William Pitt /Waugh/",   "1 SEX M",   "1 BURI",   "2 PLAC Smithey Cemetery, Wilkes County, NC",   "2 MAP",   "3 LATI N36.1548",   "3 LONG W81.1845",   "0 TRLR" ) tmp_coord <- tempfile(fileext = ".ged") writeLines(coord_ged, tmp_coord)  ped_raw <- readGedcom(tmp_coord, remove_empty_cols = FALSE, verbose = FALSE) ped_conv <- convertGedcomCoords(ped_raw) ped_raw[, c("name", "burial_lat", "burial_long")] #>                 name burial_lat burial_long #> 1 William Pitt Waugh   N36.1548    W81.1845 ped_conv[, c("name", "burial_lat", "burial_long")] #>                 name burial_lat burial_long #> 1 William Pitt Waugh    36.1548    -81.1845 unlink(tmp_coord) gedcomLat2Numeric(c("N36.1548", "S33.8688", NA)) #> [1]  36.1548 -33.8688       NA gedcomLon2Numeric(c("W81.1845", "E151.2093", NA)) #> [1] -81.1845 151.2093       NA

title: Getting Started with tidygedcom
text: data tidy data frame, BGmisc package provides next layer analysis: computing pairwise relatedness, tracing paternal lineages, identifying Y-chromosome carriers. Please see vignette “GEDCOM pedigree analysis” longer walkthrough steps using W. Henderson Waugh family tree case study.
code: library(BGmisc) #>  #> Attaching package: 'BGmisc' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:tidygedcom': #>  #>     buildTreeGrid, getWikiTreeSummary, readGed, readgedcom, readGedcom, #>     readWikifamilytree, royal92, traceTreePaths  ped <- readGedcom(tmp_ged, verbose = FALSE)  # Check the pedigree is acyclic and well-formed checks <- checkPedigreeNetwork(ped,   personID = "personID", momID = "momID", dadID = "dadID",   verbose = FALSE ) checks$is_acyclic #> [1] TRUE

title: Getting Started with tidygedcom
text: can also visualize family tree ggpedigree package. config argument allows customize plot wide range options.  Please see vignettes ggpedigree package customizing pedigree diagrams ggpedigree().
code: library(BGmisc) library(ggpedigree) ped <- readGedcom(tmp_ged, verbose = FALSE)  ggpedigree(   ped,   personID = "personID",   momID = "momID",   dadID = "dadID",   sexVar = "sex",   config = list(     label_include = TRUE,     code_male = "M",     code_female = "F",     label_column = "name",     label_text_size = 2,     focal_fill_include = TRUE,     sex_color_include = F,     focal_fill_personID = 1,     focal_fill_method = "viridis_c",     segment_lineage_include = TRUE,     segment_lineage_focal_personID = 1,     segment_lineage_component = "paternal",     segment_lineage_legend_title = "Patriline",     add_phantoms = TRUE   ) ) #> REPAIR IN EARLY ALPHA ## Cleaning up unlink(tmp_ged)

title: Read a GEDCOM File — readGedcom
text: `readGedcom()` line-oriented parser tuned common GEDCOM 5.5 5.5.1 structures. Individual records identified blocks begin `@ INDI` line. individual block passed internal parser uses simple GEDCOM tag pattern matches extract identifiers, names, life events, attributes, family relationships. Name information parsed primarily GEDCOM `NAME` tag, often encodes given names surnames using slash-delimited surname notation, `NAME John /Smith/`. parser extracts given name, surname, cleaned full name. Additional name components parsed present, including name prefix, name suffix, nickname, married surname. Birth death events recognized `BIRT` `DEAT` tags. Event details parsed collecting child lines whose GEDCOM level equals event level plus one (direct children), looking sub-fields tag name. `DATE`, `PLAC`, `CAUS` matched direct children event. Coordinates (`LATI` `LONG`) searched across descendant lines, allows located whether appear direct children (common GEDCOM 5.5.x exporters), `PLAC` (standard GEDCOM 5.5.1), `MAP` substructure `PLAC` (GEDCOM 7.x). Missing sub-fields leave corresponding output columns `NA`. Attribute tags `OCCU`, `EDUC`, `RELI`, `CAST`, `NCHI`, `NMR`, `NATI`, `RESI`, `PROP`, `SSN`, `TITL`, `DSCR`, `IDNO` parsed directly dedicated columns prefixed `attribute_`. Family relationships parsed `FAMC` `FAMS` tags. `FAMC` identifies family individual child, `FAMS` identifies families individual spouse. raw family identifiers retained parsed output unless removed post-processing. `add_parents = TRUE`, also used infer `momID` `dadID`. `post_process = TRUE`, `readGedcom()` applies optional cleanup steps controlled `add_parents`, `combine_cols`, `remove_empty_cols`, `skinny`. steps can infer parent IDs, collapse redundant name fields, remove columns entirely missing, drop raw family relationship columns slimmer output.
